reasoning: C is strongest: it explicitly maps the argument's hidden premises, enumerates the full five-causal-stories space for the correlation, names the headcount-arithmetic mechanism, demands a 2019 (not 2020) baseline, and gives a quantified confidence (5-10%) plus a falsifiability/hard-to-vary test, which is the most disciplined calibration of the three. B is very close and arguably equal on rigor, hitting post hoc, the revenue-per-head/headcount conflation, survivorship bias, false dichotomy, reverse causation, and a clean cost-benefit and verdict, but it stops short of C's explicit confidence quantification and 2019-vs-2020 baseline precision. A is solid and covers causal overreach, metric weakness, confounding, the counterfactual, remedy non-sequitur, and heterogeneity, but it is less structured, names fewer fallacies by mechanism, never flags survivorship/cherry-pick as sharply, and offers no calibrated confidence, leaving it third.
